Aditya Birla SL Midcap Fund - (G)29 names · 95.0% deployed · 86 trades · turnover 149.4%
Risk-on momentum-led midcap, leaning into names where price momentum and rising analyst revisions agree; pruning refuted laggards and signal-less corporate-action fragments.
As a momentum-led midcap book benchmarked to NIFTY MIDCAP 100, my edge is owning the cohort where two independent forces line up: cross-sectional price momentum and rising analyst-earnings revisions (treated as a small ~1-6 month tilt, not a guarantee). I keep the desk's brain-score ranking as a backbone but apply real judgment in three ways. First, I lean into corroborated leaders (strong momentum AND rising revisions) in industrials/electricals, pharma, materials and select consumer-tech, but I deliberately cap names whose momentum z is a blow-off (e.g. the telecom-equipment leader) rather than chase the quant's outsized weight. Second, I refuse to put non-equity commodity silver ETFs into an equity-selection midcap book just because they screen on a momentum z — the equity floor is met with real businesses. Third, I prune the bottom of my own book: names where revisions are clearly falling and momentum has rolled over, plus the cluster of signal-less Vedanta demerger fragments that carry no momentum, no valuation and no revision read and merely inflate my name count and single-situation risk. Net effect: a diversified ~55-name book, sectors kept well inside the 35% cap, conviction sized to signal corroboration not to any single force.

Aditya Birla SL PSU Equity Fund (G)25 names · 95.0% deployed · 44 trades · turnover 96.8%
Risk-on PSU/resource core, but rotate out of negative-revision oil-marketing & lagging PSU cyclicals and use the diversified mandate to add high-revision large/mid names for breadth.
This is a hot-beta (1.17) PSU-rooted fund whose recent edge came from resource and PSU-financial cyclicals, but the signals now split the old book sharply: the high-quality resource core (ONGC, NMDC, Coal India, Nalco, Gujarat Gas, LICI) still has strong/rising analyst revisions and cheap valuations and I keep it, while the oil-marketing companies, city-gas distributors and several lagging PSU industrials (HPCL, BPCL, IOC, MGL, IGL, BEML, BHEL, Concor, IRCTC, PNB) sit in the bottom decile of revisions with no momentum confirmation — these are theses the market is refuting and I cut or exit them. Because the encoded mandate is genuinely diversified (no PSU restriction, sector cap 100%), I exercise that freedom to add breadth via high-revision, liquid large/mid names outside the old concentration (Grasim, Coforge, Asian Paints, Muthoot, Gland, GE Shipping, Chennai Petro, Solar Industries), keeping single small-cap sizes modest. ARM is treated as a small ~1-6 month tilt (IC ~0.05), synthesized with valuation, momentum and liquidity — not chased as a basket of micro-cap revision spikes, which is why I decline most of the small-cap top of the quant baseline.

Aditya Birla SL Consumption Fund - Regular (G)36 names · 95.0% deployed · 68 trades · turnover 115.7%
Risk-on, momentum-led: rotate the book out of dead-momentum mega-caps into high-conviction momentum + rising-revision mid/small names within the theme, while keeping the structural consumption winners that still have strong analyst support.
My brain is momentum-led and my track record (IR ~1.1) was earned by riding price momentum confirmed by analyst-revision strength. The current book has drifted into large, defensive consumption and financial names where momentum has gone flat-to-negative and analyst revisions are middling or falling (HDFCBANK, ITC, MARUTI, United Spirits, Godrej, Bajaj Finserv, Tata Motors). This round I redeploy that capital decisively into the parts of my theme universe where price momentum is strong AND revisions are rising together — the telecom-equipment / defence-industrials / specialty-healthcare complex (STLTECH, MTARTECH, GE Shipping, Atlanta Electricals, Sansera, CP Plus, Acutaas) — and I keep the consumption/retail names whose momentum has cooled but whose revisions remain strong (Lenskart, Nykaa, Asian Paints, Trent, DMart). I treat ARM as a small confirming tilt, never a standalone bet: I demand momentum AND revisions to size up, and I avoid names where the two disagree (e.g. Schneider, Avanti, Jindal Saw have high momentum but collapsing revisions — I pass). Net: a more concentrated, higher-momentum book of ~36 names, still diversified across all six theme sectors, sized for conviction inside the 12% cap.

Aditya Birla SL Large & Mid Cap Fund (G)54 names · 95.0% deployed · 94 trades · turnover 96.5%
Risk-on but beta-aware: overweight the India capex / power-T&D / electrification cycle and select rising-revision metals, funded by exiting refuted cyclicals and the illiquid Vedanta demerger stubs.
This is an early round with no prior grade, so I pre-register cleanly. My edge view: the strongest, most-corroborated theme in the candidate set is India's capital-goods / power-transmission / electrification capex cycle — names where rising analyst revisions, peer-beating price momentum AND a genuine multi-year structural demand story line up (MTAR, GE Vernova T&D, Hitachi Energy, TD Power, Siemens Energy, Solar Industries, CG Power). I anchor my biggest overweight there as structural. I add a measured, revision-led metals sleeve (National Aluminium, NMDC, Hindustan Zinc, Vedanta, Hindalco) but deliberately cap it well under the 35% sector ceiling because metals is cyclical and I am already running benchmark beta of ~1.17. I ride the telecom-equipment / data-centre momentum (Sterlite Tech, HFCL) but size it for its volatility — these are tactical, not max-size forever-holds. I keep a quality-compounder ballast of rising-revision defensives (Gland, Zydus, LIC, Muthoot, Nestle, ICICI Bank) to temper beta. I fund all of this by exiting names my own falsifiers flag — collapsing revisions plus weak momentum with no offsetting view (ABB, JSW Energy, Tata Motors, Ambuja, L&T, HPCL, Max Financial, Fortis, Max Health, Sona, Paytm, GMR, Mankind) — and by dropping the illiquid, no-signal Vedanta demerger stubs (VAML, VOGL, VEDPOWER, VISL) that add single-issuer Vedanta concentration and zero analytics. I explicitly will NOT hold the silver ETFs the quant baseline ranks highly: passive bullion is not a stock-selection thesis and sits outside this Large&Mid equity mandate's spirit. Net: ~54 names, tilted to structural capex + measured cyclicals, beta-aware, no single signal chased.

Aditya Birla SL Small Cap Fund (G)60 names · 95.0% deployed · 114 trades · turnover 108.4%
Risk-on within small-caps: a breadth-heavy, analyst-revision-tilted momentum book; prune the falling-revision tail.
This is a momentum-led small-cap sleeve with a genuinely strong record (information ratio ~1.0, ~6.6pp annual excess), so the job this round is to keep that engine running, not to reinvent it. In this candidate set the price-momentum and valuation z-scores are nearly flat across the real small-caps (almost everything sits within +/-0.07 on momentum), so the only signal carrying real cross-sectional information is analyst-revision momentum (the StarMine-style upgrade/downgrade tilt). I therefore tilt the book toward names where revisions are strong or rising, hold my existing positions where revisions still support them, and exit the tail where revisions have clearly rolled over. I deliberately ignore a handful of names showing extreme momentum or cheapness scores (NIRLON, HAWKINCOOK, STLNETWORK, the Kotak IT ETF line, ZFSTEERING, WIMPLAST, BUILDPRO) because those readings look like illiquid or non-core data artifacts, not real edges — chasing a single lit-up signal on a thin name is exactly the trap to avoid. The result is a diversified ~70-name book (inside the 55-90 band, below the 90 ceiling to cut needless churn) with no single name near the 5% cap and no sector near the 30% cap; in small-caps, breadth itself is the primary risk control, and revision-momentum is treated as a small ~1-6 month tilt (IC ~0.05), never a guarantee.

Aditya Birla SL Digital India Fund (G)28 names · 95.0% deployed · 46 trades · turnover 103.1%
Risk-on within the digital theme: rotate from rolled-over IT-services into digital-infrastructure, exchanges/fintech and digital-platform winners with rising analyst revisions.
This is a Digital India thematic book and I am keeping it recognizably digital rather than letting the momentum_led signal drag it into a generic capital-goods/textiles momentum fund. The signals say two things this round: (1) the classic large/mid IT-services basket (TCS, INFY, HCL, LTI, Persistent, KPIT, Cyient, Sonata, Mastek) has rolled over on price, but analyst revisions split the group — Coforge, Hexaware, TCS, Tech Mahindra still carry rising revisions while HCL, Cyient, KPIT, Sonata, Mastek, LatentView have falling price AND falling revisions; (2) the strongest momentum-plus-rising-revision opportunities sit in digital infrastructure (optical/telecom networking), digital exchanges and fintech platforms, and digital-first consumer/gaming platforms. So I exit the names my own falsifier flags (weak momentum and weak/falling revisions), hold a trimmed IT-services core where revisions are still turning up (a forward tell even as price lags), and add digital-infra/exchange/fintech/platform leaders. I keep a small, deliberate sleeve of digital-hardware/electronics-manufacturing names (surveillance, defense electronics, EMS) as Digital India hardware exposure, but cap theme drift hard. ARM-style revision strength is treated as a small 1-6 month tilt, never a guarantee, and is synthesized with momentum, valuation and the existing book — no single signal is chased.

Aditya Birla SL Banking&Financial Services (G)31 names · 95.0% deployed · 50 trades · turnover 79.4%
Risk-on momentum-led BFSI; overweight exchanges and rising-revision lenders/insurers, cut the falling-revision laggards.
This is a momentum-led Banking & Financial Services sleeve, and the cleanest edge right now sits in the market-infrastructure and lender names where strong price momentum, rising analyst revisions and (in several cases) cheap valuations all point the same way. I am leaning hardest into the exchange/market-structure leaders (MCX, BSE) where momentum is exceptional and revisions are firming, and into the rare triple-confirmed names like Karnataka Bank that are simultaneously cheap, trending and being upgraded. I keep a spread of recovering small-finance banks, gold-financiers and revision-led insurers to harvest breadth (more independent bets improves the law-of-active-management √BR term), while keeping large-cap quality anchors (ICICI Bank, SBI, Axis) at modest weights for ballast rather than as the alpha source. I am systematically exiting names my own signals refute: falling-revision, weak-momentum holdings (RBL, 360 ONE, Canara Robeco AMC, HDB Financial, Go Digit, Prudent) and momentum-laggard mega-caps (HDFC Bank, HDFC Life, Bajaj Finserv, Bajaj Finance) that do not earn their place in a momentum sleeve. Net: same low-beta, alpha-seeking posture that has produced the fund's ~6% excess CAGR, expressed through a tighter, higher-conviction roster.

Pre-registered bets — thesis & falsifier (anti-hindsight)
MCX cyclical 5.9%
MCX shows the strongest momentum in the universe alongside rising analyst revisions; a commodity-derivatives volume upcycle and product launches drive a high-margin, asset-light earnings ramp.
✗ wrong if: Momentum z collapses toward zero or revisions roll over, or monthly traded-volume growth stalls for two consecutive months.
BSE cyclical 5.2%
BSE compounds on derivatives/option volume share gains with strong momentum and rising revisions; operating leverage on a fixed cost base lifts margins.
✗ wrong if: Derivatives volume share reverses or revisions turn down, or momentum z falls below 1.
KTKBANK structural 4.9%
Karnataka Bank is simultaneously cheap on earnings/book, trending, and being upgraded — a re-rating candidate as ROA normalizes; multi-signal confirmation lowers single-factor risk.
✗ wrong if: Asset-quality slippage (rising GNPA/credit cost) or revisions fade, removing the upgrade leg of the thesis.
MAHABANK structural 4.9%
Bank of Maharashtra retains strong relative momentum and reasonable valuation; PSU re-rating intact but revision support is moderate, so I size it large yet trimmed from 5.2%.
✗ wrong if: Momentum z drops below 1 or deposit-growth/margin pressure forces estimate downgrades.
PNBHOUSING structural 4.3%
PNB Housing keeps positive momentum and firming revisions as it re-accelerates retail housing AUM with improving asset quality.
✗ wrong if: Revisions turn negative or AUM growth/spreads compress, breaking the re-acceleration thesis.
INDUSINDBK tactical 3.7%
IndusInd is recovering with rising revisions, improving momentum and value support; stabilization in microfinance/credit cost drives a re-rating off a low base.
✗ wrong if: Renewed asset-quality shock or governance concern, or revisions reverse down.

Aditya Birla SL MNC Fund - (G)41 names · 95.0% deployed · 64 trades · turnover 90.3%
Risk-on within a diversified mandate; lean the book toward names with rising analyst revisions, prune the refuted low-revision legacy positions, and avoid thin-data momentum/value mirages.
This round the live cross-section gives me only one usable edge: analyst-revision momentum (ARM). Price-momentum and valuation z-scores are flat-to-zero across almost the entire universe (the handful of large positive readings sit in illiquid micro-caps like NIRLON, HAWKINCOOK, STLNETWORK, BUILDPRO and ETF-style tickers, which I read as thin-data artifacts, not real signal), and net-active flow is null everywhere. So my honest move is to tilt the book — gently, since ARM is a ~1-6 month effect with IC near 0.05, never a guarantee — toward names where analysts are upgrading, while keeping my genuinely-held franchise positions that still carry neutral-or-better revisions, and exiting the legacy positions the revision signal now refutes (Siemens, ABB, RBL Bank, LG Electronics, Sumitomo Chem, Timken, Fortis, United Spirits, DOMS, Bayer, 360ONE, Linde, Nippon AMC — all deep in the falling-revision zone). I add a spread of fresh strong-revision candidates (ONGC, NMDC, Muthoot, LICI, Grasim, Gland, Coforge, Solar Inds, Lenskart, Equitas, GE Shipping, Chambal, Asian Paints) to broaden the bets and lift effective breadth rather than concentrate. Cap-bucket mix stays diversified (large/mid/small). I will not over-size any single name; conviction tops out around 3% so the guardrail's 12% cap is never binding, and I keep ~36 names to stay mid-band of the 25-50 mandate. This is synthesis of a validated weak signal, not manufactured alpha.

Risk-on, momentum-led; rotate within the book toward names with rising analyst revisions and widen breadth, while dialing back unscored single-issuer and falling-revision exposure.
This is an early round with no graded track record yet, so I anchor on what the signals actually say. Price momentum and valuation are cross-sectionally flat across almost the whole universe this round (the only extreme z-scores sit on illiquid micro-caps that look like stale-print artifacts, not real edge), so the one differentiating, well-covered force is analyst-revision momentum (ARM). I treat ARM as a small ~1-6 month tilt (IC ~0.05), not a guarantee, and use it to (a) rotate within sleeves — out of low-revision PSU banks and falling-revision OMCs/staples into rising-revision NBFCs, insurers, gas, healthcare and select industrials — and (b) widen breadth across sectors, since my realized info ratio (0.55) sits on a high beta (1.16), meaning past excess return leaned on market leverage rather than many independent bets. I keep my liquid, still-supported cyclical core (Vedanta parent, metals, autos, cables/industrials) but cut the four unscored Vedanta demerger shells, which are a concentrated single-issuer bet with no signal behind them. Net: ~42 names, diversified, momentum-led, tilted to where analysts are upgrading, with no name near the 12% cap.

Risk-aware quality tilt: rotate toward liquid, rising-revision cyclicals and quality compounders; cut the redundant Vedanta cluster and falling-revision laggards; bring beta back toward 1.
This is an early round with no graded track record, so I anchor on signal discipline rather than re-chasing past calls. Two things define this rebalance: (1) cross-sectional price momentum is compressed this period — nearly every name's z_mom is near zero, with the only large readings sitting in illiquid micro-caps and odd ETF/penny tickers I refuse to chase — so the brain's momentum leg gives little differentiation; (2) the live edge is the analyst-revision (ARM) tilt, which I treat as a small ~1-6 month overlay (IC ~0.05), never a guarantee. I therefore lean the book toward liquid large/mid names with strong or rising revisions and reasonable valuation (energy/PSU value like ONGC, NMDC, Gujarat Gas; quality compounders Grasim, Asian Paints, Coforge, Gland; plus held winners Muthoot, Lenskart, P&G Health), while cutting two pockets of risk: the redundant Vedanta demerger complex (five separate listed entities that are one concentrated bet dressed as diversification, with no revision coverage on the sub-entities) which I collapse to a single small VEDL expression, and the falling-revision laggards (Sona, APL Apollo, Adani Power, Shriram, SJS, Delhivery) where the brain and revisions both point down. Net effect: ~30-34 names inside the band, beta nudged down from 1.16, conviction concentrated in liquid names where I can actually defend the thesis.

Aditya Birla SL Conglomerate Fund - Regular (G)37 names · 95.0% deployed · 66 trades · turnover 96.5%
Risk-on but quality-tilted momentum book: ride rising analyst revisions, prune the refuted cyclicals, keep beta from drifting higher.
This is a momentum-led, diversified ("Conglomerate") book benchmarked to the NIFTY 500, and this round the only signal with real cross-sectional discrimination is analyst-revision momentum (ARM): price-momentum (z_mom) and value (z_val) are essentially flat across the universe, and net-flow is unavailable. So I lean the book toward names where analysts are actively upgrading earnings (strong/rising revisions), treated as a SMALL 1-6 month tilt (IC ~0.05), not a guarantee. I keep my proven quality-cyclical anchors where revisions are still constructive (metals/industrials/financials/IT large caps), rotate fresh capital into high-revision energy, materials, financials and capital-goods names, and EXIT the cyclicals the revision signal has clearly turned against (Adani infra/energy-solutions, L&T, JSW Energy, Ambuja, Voltas, CEAT, cement/chemical laggards) plus the Vedanta demerger fragments that carry no signal at all. My track record (IR 0.59 but beta 1.16) warns that my edge has come with too much beta, so I deliberately do not add more deep-cyclical leverage — I tilt toward revision-supported quality instead. I explicitly refuse to chase the two top-brain-score names (NIRLON, HAWKINS) whose scores are inflated by an implausible single momentum z-score that looks like a data artifact, and I ignore non-stock/ETF tickers.

Risk-on within theme: rotate hard from signal-dead legacy auto mega-caps into momentum + rising-revision industrials/auto-tech leaders.
This is a momentum-led thematic book and the signals are unusually clear this round: my largest legacy positions (the big two-wheeler/four-wheeler OEMs, the commodity tyre/port/CV names) cluster at the very bottom of the desk's blended score with flat-to-weak price momentum and flat-to-falling analyst revisions, while a cohort of industrials and auto-tech/precision names at the top of the universe pair the strongest peer-relative momentum with the strongest rising analyst revisions — and I own none of them. So I rotate decisively: concentrate conviction in MTARTECH, GESHIP, ATLANTAELE, TDPOWERSYS, CPPLUS and a basket of strong-momentum/rising-revision industrials (WELCORP, AVALON, RRKABEL, ASTRAMICRO, ENGINERSIN, AIAENG) plus theme-fit auto-components leaders (SANSERA, BHARATFORG, TENNIND), keep a measured sleeve of structural compounders where the franchise view survives even if near-term momentum is cooling (M&M, EICHERMOT, MARUTI, MOTHERSON), and exit the names the data refutes (SWIGGY, INDIGO, CEATLTD, ASHOKLEY, ADANIPORTS, TMCV, GABRIEL, ASAHIINDIA, SONACOMS, HEROMOTOCO, TIINDIA, MINDACORP). ARM is treated as a small 1-6 month tilt that I require to AGREE with momentum and flows before I size up — never as a standalone bet. Net: same theme, but the book is repositioned onto where revisions and price are both improving.

Aditya Birla SL Dividend Yield Fund (G)46 names · 95.0% deployed · 60 trades · turnover 61.7%
Risk-balanced value-with-rising-revisions; overweight cheap dividend cash-cows (energy/PSU/financials) where analysts are upgrading, exit the names where revisions have collapsed.
This is a Dividend Yield Fund run on a value_revision brain, so the durable edge is owning cheap, cash-generative dividend payers AT THE MOMENT analyst earnings revisions turn up — the combination, not either signal alone. I tilt the book toward names that screen cheap (positive cheapness z) AND carry strong or rising analyst revisions (ONGC, NMDC, COALINDIA, LICI, Gujarat Gas, Chennai Petroleum, Chambal Fertilizers, Muthoot, GE Shipping, Karnataka Bank, plus quality compounders Grasim/Asian Paints whose revisions have firmed), keep large-cap dividend ballast (Power Grid, NTPC, SBI, Axis, HDFC Bank, ITC, Bajaj Auto, BEL) for benchmark beta control, and decisively cut the held names whose revisions have rolled over hard (HINDPETRO, BPCL, MGL, Emami, Britannia, Dhanuka). I explicitly refuse the quant baseline's top pick STLNETWORK and the other extreme-cheapness microcaps (BUILDPRO/ADVENTHTL/GENSOL/JPOLYINVST) — their cheapness z-scores are statistical artifacts (no analyst coverage, broken ratios), not real value. I also collapse the four synthetic Vedanta demerger stubs (no independent signal) back into the parent VEDL. Net: a diversified ~45-name book, broadly within sector caps, leaning into cheap-and-upgrading, out of cheap-and-deteriorating.

Risk-on momentum-led manufacturing book: concentrate where price strength is confirmed by rising analyst revisions; cut momentum-dead and revision-falling legacy names.
This is a momentum-led manufacturing-theme book, so my edge is buying names where strong 6-12 month price momentum is CONFIRMED by rising analyst earnings revisions and a not-overly-stretched valuation - the "triple confirm" - and refusing momentum that analysts are deserting. I am tilting the book toward capital-goods/electricals, non-ferrous metals and selective specialty healthcare/chemicals, where the manufacturing/industrial capex cycle, power-grid build-out and import-substitution themes are showing both accelerating price action and upward revision pressure. I deliberately concentrate the top of the book (MTAR, GE Shipping, National Aluminium, Vedanta, Atlanta Electricals) into the highest-conviction confirmed-momentum names rather than spreading thinly, while keeping 35+ holdings for breadth so no single bet dominates. I am exiting a long tail of legacy holdings whose price momentum has rolled over (Reliance, V-Guard, Blue Star, Balkrishna, Asian Paints, BEL) or where analyst revisions have turned down even as price held (Adani Power, Sona, Praj, Techno Electric) - for a momentum-led desk, unconfirmed or fading momentum is exactly where the bleed comes from. ARM (analyst-revision) signal is used only as a small confirming tilt, never as a standalone reason to own a name. Treat all signals as noisy; the book is a diversified bet on the joint signal, not on any one ticker.

Core resources position confirmed by both price and revisions; demerger optionality.
✗ wrong if: Commodity rollover plus revision downgrades, or balance-sheet/governance shock at the group level.
ATLANTAELE structural 3.2%
Direct play on power-transmission capex with both signals confirming.
✗ wrong if: Order-book/revision slowdown or momentum z-score collapsing below peers.
CPPLUS structural 3.1%
High-revision industrial with confirming price action - electronics import-substitution theme.
✗ wrong if: Revisions decelerating or a momentum break; margin compression from component-cost inflation.
⚙ guardrail: deployed 95.0% across 37 names (cap 12%/name, 100%/sector)
Aditya Birla SL Infrastructure Fund - (G)38 names · 95.0% deployed · 72 trades · turnover 119.3%
Risk-on within the infra theme: lean back into strong-momentum + rising-analyst-revision capex/defense/T&D winners, prune the decayed low-momentum tail.
This is a momentum-led infrastructure thematic book whose edge has historically come from owning names where price momentum and analyst-revision momentum point the same way. The current 50-name book had drifted into a long tail of holdings where momentum has rolled over AND analysts are cutting estimates (NCC, GMR Airport, Ahluwalia, Siemens, Voltamp, Praj, HPCL, Adani Ports, ABB, Sona). I am cutting that tail and concentrating into the live infra/capex up-cycle where both forces align: power transmission & distribution equipment, defense electronics, metals/mining with rising revisions, EMS/electronics, and exchanges. I keep the structural winners I already own that still score well (Vedanta, Sansera, TD Power, Hindalco, Thermax) and trim a few low-conviction large-cap financial/energy anchors. I deliberately do NOT max-size the two parabolic names (Sterlite Tech, MTAR) despite the strongest signals, because momentum that extreme tends to mean-revert — I take meaningful but capped positions. Net: a tighter, higher-conviction infra book tilted to where the analyst-and-price evidence agrees.

⚙ guardrail: deployed 95.0% across 38 names (cap 12%/name, 100%/sector)
Aditya Birla SL Equity Savings Fund (G)38 names · 35.0% deployed · 72 trades · turnover 39.6%
Conservative risk-on within the hedged Equity-Savings mandate: tilt the equity sleeve toward where analyst-revision, momentum and value align — overweight Energy/Materials cyclicals and high-revision Financials; cut refuted laggards.
This is an Equity Savings fund (20-50% net equity, the rest hedged/cash) currently sitting on a flat, diffuse 55-name book at 65% cash with a negative information ratio (-0.39) and very low beta (0.41) — i.e. it has been under-taking active risk and underperforming the NIFTY 500. With no prior round to grade (last_round_review is null) and net-active flow unavailable, I synthesize the signals I do trust: LSEG StarMine analyst-revision strength (a small 1-6 month tilt, IC~0.05 — used directionally, never as a guarantee), cross-sectional 6m-1m momentum, cross-sectional cheapness, and this desk's regime_switch brain blend. The edge cluster this round is clear and corroborated across signals: Energy (ONGC, Chennai Petro, Coal India, Oil India) and Materials (NMDC, Hindalco, National Aluminium, Hindustan Zinc, Grasim) carry strong, rising revisions AND attractive relative value AND firm momentum — a genuine multi-force confirmation rather than a single-signal chase. I lift those to real conviction, keep high-revision Financials (Muthoot, LICI, IndusInd) and quality compounders, and EXIT the names my own falsifiers flag: deep-laggard low-revision holdings (Tata Motors CV/PV, Adani Ports, RBL, Tata Power, Vodafone Idea, UltraTech) where falling revisions and weak momentum coincide. The result is a tighter, more conviction-weighted equity sleeve (~40 names, within the 30-55 band) that should restore active risk in a defensible, signal-led way while staying inside per-name (7%) and per-sector (35%) caps after the guardrail.

⚙ guardrail: deployed 35.0% across 38 names (cap 7%/name, 35%/sector)
Aditya Birla SL Special Opportunities Fund (G)47 names · 95.0% deployed · 59 trades · turnover 49.6%
Risk-on, momentum-led but quality-tilted: lean into rising analyst-revision names, prune the dead post-demerger stubs and falling-revision laggards.
This is an early round with no prior scorecard to learn from, so I anchor on signal structure and book continuity rather than hindsight. The one signal carrying real cross-sectional information this round is analyst-revision momentum (ARM): price-momentum z-scores are essentially flat across the whole universe and value z-scores are near-zero for most names, so the deterministic baseline is effectively an ARM ranking. I treat ARM as exactly what it is — a small 1-6 month tilt with modest hit-rate, not a guarantee — so I tilt the book toward names where analysts are clearly upgrading while refusing to chase any single name to the cap. Three concrete moves define the book: (1) EXIT the Vedanta post-demerger stubs (the iron/steel, power, oil, aluminium spin entities) which carry no revisions, no momentum and a flat placeholder score, and which together concentrated ~10% of the book in one corporate family with no signal; (2) cut or trim the clear falling-revision laggards I hold (autos, one stressed private bank, an infra name, a couple of weak-revision financials) where there is no value or momentum offset; (3) recycle that capital into rising-revision names that also pass a sanity check on liquidity and cheapness — large/mid energy and materials (ONGC, NMDC, Chennai Petro), select financials with improving revisions (LICI, Muthoot), quality compounders being upgraded (Asian Paints, Grasim, Coforge, Solar Industries) and a deep-value telecom-equipment turnaround. The book stays high-beta and momentum-led, consistent with the desk's strong historical excess return, but is cleaner and more diversified across the revision-leaders.

Aditya Birla SL Pharma & Healthcare Fund (G)34 names · 95.0% deployed · 47 trades · turnover 74.2%
Risk-on within Healthcare: overweight names where strong price momentum AND rising analyst revisions agree; cut late-momentum names where revisions are falling.
This is a momentum-led, historically low-beta/high-information-ratio pharma & healthcare book, and I am keeping that character. The edge in pharma comes from riding pipeline, USFDA-approval and earnings-revision cycles, so I tilt hardest where two independent forces line up: strong cross-sectional price momentum AND rising analyst revisions, with valuation as a tie-breaker and a guard against chasing. I lead with the CDMO/CRAMS and complex-generics names that are clearly working (Acutaas, Gland, Rubicon, Granules, Laurus, Natco, Zydus, Marksans) and anchor the book with a large-cap quality core (Apollo, Sun, Cipla) plus a diagnostics sleeve (Vijaya, Thyrocare, Metropolis, Dr Lal). I am deliberately fading three traps: (1) names showing strong price momentum but sharply FALLING revisions (Shilpa, Emcure) — classic late-momentum reversals; (2) the defensive MNC-pharma cluster (Abbott, GSK, Pfizer, Sanofi) which is dead weight in a momentum book; and (3) the single-signal outliers the quant overweights on noise (Blue Jet, whose score rests entirely on an implausible cheapness reading with negative momentum). Net flows are unavailable this round, so I lean on momentum + revisions + valuation only. ARM is treated as a small 1-6 month tilt, never a guarantee.

Para-IV/complex-generics optionality that is both cheap relative to peers and showing strong momentum with rising revisions — the best risk/reward where value and momentum agree.

Aditya Birla SL ESG Integration Strategy Fund (G)45 names · 95.0% deployed · 58 trades · turnover 50.7%
Risk-aware, momentum-led tilt: keep a liquid quality core, lean toward names with rising analyst revisions, cut the revision-laggards, and refuse the micro-cap / single-signal artifacts the quant baseline is chasing.
This is an early round with nothing yet to grade, a diversified thematic mandate benchmarked to NIFTY 500, and a momentum-led brain. My read of the desk is that the deterministic quant baseline is over-rotating into an ARM-95-to-100 tail that is dominated by small/illiquid names and a few obvious data artifacts (extreme single-axis z-scores on tiny tickers like a +39 momentum z, a +25 cheapness z on a distressed sub-Rs30 stock). At Rs558cr AUM I cannot responsibly dump equal slugs into that wall of micro-caps, and ARM is only a small 1-to-6-month tilt (IC about 0.05), not a guarantee. So I keep my diversified quality core, express the analyst-revision tilt through LIQUID, investable names where revisions are genuinely strong (ONGC, NMDC, LICI, GRASIM, ASIANPAINT, MUTHOOTFIN, GLAND, SOLARINDS, COFORGE), and I cut or shrink the clear revision-laggards where analysts are downgrading and the quant score is weak (BRITANNIA, RBLBANK, JKCEMENT, DALBHARAT, SONACOMS, INDUSTOWER, GODREJCP). Net effect: a slightly more concentrated, higher-revision, more liquid book than the starting 50, staying well inside the mandate's spirit (about 42 names, no name near the 12% cap, broad sector spread).

⚙ guardrail: deployed 95.0% across 45 names (cap 12%/name, 100%/sector)
How to read this. An experimental, paper-money research firm: agents — sector analysts, fund managers, a CIO — read the terminal's real data and produce grounded views. Every view cites the actual numbers (ARM 0–100 analyst-revision momentum, net fund flows in ₹cr, growth, real fund IR/IC). Agents synthesize validated signals into judgment; they do not manufacture alpha. Skill is judged by the Fundamental Law of Active Management (IR = IC·√breadth·TC): analysts on IC, managers on IR (and the transfer-coefficient leak), the firm on breadth. Not investment advice. Generated 2026-06-26 · data as of 2026-05.
🔬 IT & Technology Sector Analyst
constructive
Coverage 33 stocks · 22 recommending · Sector ARM 57.6 equal-wt / 62.4 float-wt
IT desk is selectively constructive: float-weighted sector ARM 62.4 leads equal-weight 57.6, so analyst upgrades are concentrated in the large/quality names — the edge is in mid-cap quality compounders (Coforge, Hexaware, Mphasis) where ARM, fund flow and PAT all confirm, not in the broad sector.
Working on
  • Reconciling the ARM-vs-flow split in Quadrant 2 (11 names where analysts are upgrading but funds are net sellers — OFSS, LTM, LTTS, Wipro): is this funds rotating into mid-caps, or a leading sell signal the Street hasn't caught?
  • Pressure-testing the Wipro distribution: -4,985.8 Rs cr fund outflow against a still-OK ARM 61 — flagging whether ARM is stale on a name funds have clearly abandoned.
  • Watching the high-growth / low-ARM cohort (Persistent PAT +33.1% / ARM 46, IKS +48.6% / ARM 68, Tata Elxsi +41.1% / ARM 66) — growth is real but analysts are NOT yet revising up; the revision clock has not started.
  • Quality-control on laggards KPIT (PAT -24.2%, ARM 24), Cyient (-28.7%, ARM 25) and HCLTech (-4.3%, ARM 26) — confirming these stay off the buy list even though HCLTech shows fund inflow of +437.6 Rs cr.
Pitches
accumulate COFORGE ●●●1-3M→ Mid Cap, Large & Mid Cap, Flexi Cap, Technology & Digital, CIO
Cleanest triple-confirm in the sector: top ARM, heavy fund accumulation and explosive earnings all align — a mid-cap quality compounder the Street is actively upgrading.
ARM 97 (sector-best), net fund flow +942.6 Rs cr (3rd-highest in coverage), PAT YoY +86.3% (growth leader), FII +24.66%, Quadrant 1. Mcap 59,303 cr.
accumulate HEXT ●●●1-3M→ Mid Cap, Large & Mid Cap, Technology & Digital
Mid-cap IT with full Quadrant-1 confluence — analysts upgrading, funds buying, double-digit earnings growth; cleaner risk/reward than the slow-growth large-cap majors.
ARM 88, net fund flow +462.7 Rs cr, PAT YoY +14.0%, Quadrant 1. FII +4.2% (room to build). Mcap 45,634 cr.
add TECHM ●●○1-3M→ Large Cap, Large & Mid Cap, Technology & Digital, Flexi Cap
Largest fund accumulation in the desk paired with a high ARM and a genuine earnings turnaround — the large-cap name where smart money and the Street agree most.
Net fund flow +1,061.6 Rs cr (highest in coverage), ARM 77, PAT YoY +13.0%, FII +18.59%, Quadrant 1. Mcap 147,707 cr.
add MPHASIS ●●○1-3M→ Mid Cap, Large & Mid Cap, Technology & Digital
Mid-cap Quadrant-1 confirmer — strong analyst revision, steady fund inflow and rising FII; fills out the mid-cap quality basket alongside Coforge and Hexaware.
ARM 83, net fund flow +239.8 Rs cr, PAT YoY +9.5%, FII +19.51%, Quadrant 1. Mcap 53,506 cr.
reduce WIPRO ●●○1-3M→ Large Cap, Value & Contra, Technology & Digital, CIO
Mutual funds are dumping it at scale while earnings stay flat; the still-decent ARM looks stale against the exodus — treat as a source of funds, not a value buy.
Net fund flow -4,985.8 Rs cr (by far the largest distribution in coverage), PAT YoY +0.3% (flat), ARM 61 (lagging the flow signal), Quadrant 2. Mcap 263,357 cr.
Risks
  • Sector signal is narrow: only Quadrant-1 names (11 of 33) carry real confluence; the average IT stock (equal-weight ARM 57.6) is unremarkable, so this is a stock-selection call, not a sector overweight.
  • ARM is a small tilt (IC ~0.03-0.045) on a 1-6 month clock — not a per-name guarantee; the mid-cap winners already carry rich expectations (Coforge ARM 97 / FII 24.66%, Hexaware ARM 88).
  • Mixed flow-vs-fundamentals in the large caps: TCS pairs ARM 76 and +755.8 Rs cr inflow with PAT of only +1.3%; HCLTech shows funds buying +437.6 Rs cr against ARM 26 and PAT -4.3% — large-cap IT growth is anaemic.
  • High-growth / lagging-ARM names (Persistent PAT +33.1% / ARM 46, IKS +48.6% / ARM 68 — both funds-selling) risk being value traps if the Street's caution is right; do not chase growth without the revision confirm.
🔬 Banking & Financials Sector Analyst
constructive
Coverage 111 stocks · 57 recommending · Sector ARM 55.2 equal-wt / 54.1 float-wt
Financials desk is selectively constructive, not broadly bullish: sector ARM is only middling (EW 55.2 / float-weighted 54.1) with 57 of 111 names recommending, so the edge is name-specific — concentrated in large private banks where fund flow is heaviest (ICICI, HDFC) and in a high-ARM gold-NBFC + capital-markets growth cluster (Muthoot, PB Fintech, BSE, IIFL), while several PSU and registry/AMC names are in clear analyst-and-flow downgrade.
Working on
  • Resolving the ICICI-vs-HDFC private-bank pair: ICICI is clean quadrant-1 (ARM 61, +17,128 cr flow, PAT +6.2%) vs HDFC quadrant-3 (ARM 46 but the single largest 3m flow at +13,562 cr and FII +44.05) — deciding whether HDFC's flow leads a coming analyst upgrade or is index-driven buying without revision support
  • Sizing the gold-loan / NBFC growth cluster (Muthoot ARM 97 PAT +98.1%, IIFL ARM 81 PAT +214.2%, Poonawalla ARM 98) — testing whether the ARM-plus-earnings combination is durable or a base-effect spike before routing conviction sizing
  • Flagging the LICI divergence: ARM 99 (analyst-led, quadrant 2) but funds distributing -934 cr and FII flat at 0.33 — checking whether revision momentum or smart-money flow is the better read here before I pitch it
  • Watching the PSU-bank / lender deterioration (PNB ARM 18, Bank of Baroda ARM 42 with -1,581 cr outflow, REC ARM 5) and capital-market-infra laggards (CDSL ARM 2, KFintech ARM 6) to set explicit avoids for the FM desks
Pitches
accumulate ICICIBANK ●●●3-6M→ Banking & Financials, Large Cap, Flexi Cap
Cleanest large-cap bank on the desk: positive on both forces (quadrant 1) with the heaviest active fund accumulation in the sector and supportive analyst revision momentum — a core private-bank holding to add into, not chase.
ARM 61, 3m net fund flow +17,128 cr (largest accumulated in coverage), PAT YoY +6.2%, FII change +34.48, mcap 9.99 lakh cr, quadrant 1.
add MUTHOOTFIN ●●●1-3M→ Banking & Financials, Large & Mid Cap, Mid Cap
Top-decile analyst revision momentum paired with near-doubling of profit — the gold-loan cycle is being upgraded on earnings, not hope; a quality mid/large NBFC for growth-tilted books.
ARM 97, PAT YoY +98.1%, 3m fund flow +907.6 cr (accumulating), FII change +12.31, mcap 1.25 lakh cr, quadrant 1.
add POLICYBZR ●●○1-3M→ Mid Cap, Technology & Digital, Flexi Cap
Fintech where all three forces line up — high ARM, strong active buying, and a profit inflection — a higher-beta growth expression of the financials theme for risk-on desks.
ARM 68, 3m fund flow +2,445.3 cr (top-5 accumulated), PAT YoY +89.8%, FII change +39.94, mcap 82,916 cr, quadrant 1.
add BSE ●●○1-3M→ Mid Cap, Small Cap, Quant / Systematic
Capital-markets-infra winner: high revision momentum and ~88% profit growth on the derivatives/volume cycle, with modest but positive fund buying — a smaller-cap, higher-conviction growth name for mid/small books.
ARM 77, PAT YoY +88.0%, 3m fund flow +182.4 cr, FII change +19.42, mcap 1.01 lakh cr, quadrant 1.
watch LICI ●○○3-6M→ CIO, Value & Contra, Large Cap
Highest analyst revision in coverage with solid profit growth, but funds are net sellers and FII is flat — a force divergence (quadrant 2). Given ARM's modest IC, I want flow confirmation before sizing; route to CIO because it is a large, contentious call.
ARM 99 (sector-top), PAT YoY +18.9%, but 3m fund flow -934.4 cr (net distributed), FII change only +0.33, mcap 5.65 lakh cr, quadrant 2.
Risks
  • ARM is a small tilt (IC ~0.03-0.045) on a 1-6 month clock, not a per-name guarantee — the high-ARM growth cluster (Muthoot +98%, IIFL +214%, BSE +88% PAT) carries base-effect risk where a single in-line quarter reverses the revision wave.
  • HDFCBANK is the largest flow in the sector (+13,562 cr) but quadrant 3 with ARM only 46 — that buying may be index/passive-driven rather than active conviction, so flow here should not be read as an analyst-confirmed signal.
  • Clear deterioration in PSU banks and select lenders (PNB ARM 18, Bank of Baroda ARM 42 with -1,581 cr outflow, REC ARM 5, RECLTD/Jio Financial both quadrant 4) — avoid these for the FM desks; sector breadth is uneven, not a broad rally.
  • Rate-cycle and credit-cost sensitivity: NBFC/gold-loan and insurance names (Muthoot, Poonawalla, LICI, SBI Life PAT YoY 0.0%) are exposed to a turn in funding costs or asset quality that current revision momentum has not yet priced.
🔬 Pharma & Healthcare Sector Analyst
constructive
Coverage 68 stocks · 43 recommending · Sector ARM 56.9 equal-wt / 52.8 float-wt
Pharma & Healthcare is constructive but two-speed: float-weighted ARM (52.8) lags equal-weight (56.9), meaning the index megacaps (Sun, Divi's, Cipla, Dr Reddy) are revision-neutral while the genuine analyst momentum sits in hospitals/diagnostics and a CDMO/API cohort (Anthem, Laurus, Glenmark, Gland) — favour where ARM, fund flow and PAT growth all point the same way, fade the crowded-but-stalling large generics.
Working on
  • Reconciling the ARM-vs-flow split in the index megacaps: Sun Pharma is the single biggest fund magnet (+Rs 3,069 cr 3m flow, Q1) yet ARM is only 50 and PAT +5% — testing whether that flow is conviction or passive/specialty-driven before sizing.
  • Quadrant-2 'analysts ahead of funds' watchlist (Zydus ARM 91, Lupin ARM 63, Gland ARM 96, Glenmark ARM 77, Ipca ARM 86) where ARM is high but 3m flow is negative — deciding which are early-signal accumulation candidates vs. value traps.
  • CDMO/diagnostics structural cluster — Anthem (ARM 79, +Rs 899 cr, PAT +31%), Laurus (ARM 80, PAT +148%), Thyrocare (ARM 81, PAT +79%), Fortis/Max hospitals — sizing the de-correlated growth basket for the Pharma & Healthcare and Mid/Small desks.
  • Avoid-list discipline on the Q4 ARM laggards with collapsing PAT: Biocon (ARM 10, PAT -74%, -Rs 994 cr flow), Piramal Pharma (ARM 16, PAT -458%), Cohance (ARM 1, PAT -69%) — flag not-recommending.
Pitches
accumulate ANTHEM (Anthem Biosciences) ●●●3-6M→ Mid Cap, Pharma & Healthcare, Flexi Cap
Cleanest triple-confirmation name in the desk: a CDMO where analyst revision momentum, active fund accumulation and reported growth all line up — Quadrant 1 with no offsetting red flag.
ARM 79 (top decile of coverage), 3m net fund flow +Rs 899 cr (3rd-largest accumulation in the sector after Sun and Max), PAT YoY +31.3%, mcap Rs 41,551 cr. FII chg only +1.28 so this is domestic-MF-led, not crowded foreign money.
accumulate LAURUSLABS (Laurus Labs) ●●●3-6M→ Mid Cap, Pharma & Healthcare, Quant / Systematic
Best growth-plus-momentum combination on the desk — a CDMO/API turnaround where the earnings inflection is already showing and analysts are upgrading, even though funds haven't fully chased it yet (Quadrant 2 = analysts ahead).
ARM 80, PAT YoY +147.9% (a true earnings inflection, our top growth-leader by combined ARM+growth), mcap Rs 49,354 cr; 3m flow only -Rs 65.5 cr (mild, not distribution) and FII chg +25.82 — foreign money already positioning ahead of domestic funds.
add APOLLOHOSP (Apollo Hospitals) ●●●3-6M→ Large Cap, Large & Mid Cap, Consumption, Flexi Cap
Best-aligned large-cap on the desk and the cleanest way to own the structural hospital/healthcare-delivery theme — every signal is positive at once with no PAT or flow blemish.
ARM 57 (highest among the Rs 100k cr+ names), 3m flow +Rs 618.6 cr, PAT YoY +33%, Quadrant 1, mcap Rs 107,953 cr, FII chg +42.62. Contrast with same-size generics Sun (ARM 50, PAT +5%) and Cipla (ARM 52, PAT -26.7%) — Apollo pairs growth with momentum, they don't.
watch ZYDUSLIFE (Zydus Lifesciences) ●●○1-3M→ Large Cap, Value & Contra, Flexi Cap
Strongest analyst-revision signal among the large generics, but funds are not yet buying — a Quadrant-2 'analysts ahead' early-flag to accumulate on confirmation, not a chase here.
ARM 91 (highest ARM of any Rs 90k cr+ name in the sector, vs. Sun 50 / Cipla 52 / Dr Reddy 38), PAT YoY +9.7% (positive vs. peers' declines), mcap Rs 97,840 cr. But 3m flow -Rs 159.6 cr and FII chg only +6.95 — the revision tilt has not turned into buying, so size only on a flow turn.
avoid BIOCON ●●○1-3M→ CIO, Large & Mid Cap, Value & Contra
Cleanest negative on the desk — analysts cutting, funds selling and earnings collapsing simultaneously; flag not-recommending until the triple turns.
ARM 10 (bottom of coverage), 3m flow -Rs 994.3 cr (2nd-largest distribution in the sector), PAT YoY -74.2%, Quadrant 4, mcap Rs 50,164 cr. Same Q4-laggard pattern as Piramal Pharma (ARM 16, PAT -457.7%) and Cohance (ARM 1, PAT -69%).
Risks
  • ARM is a small tilt (IC ~0.03-0.045) on a 1-6 month clock, not a per-name guarantee — these are probabilistic over a basket, not single-stock certainties; size accordingly.
  • Sun Pharma's huge +Rs 3,069 cr flow with only ARM 50 / PAT +5% may be passive/specialty-pipeline-driven rather than fundamental conviction — do not read it as a strong-buy without the revision and growth support that Apollo/Anthem have.
  • Quadrant-2 'analysts-ahead' names (Zydus, Lupin, Gland, Glenmark) carry negative 3m flow; if the revision signal is wrong, there is no fund support underneath — these can sit dead or de-rate (value-trap risk).
  • Sector is two-speed: large generics (Cipla PAT -26.7%, Dr Reddy -27.4%) are dragging float-weighted ARM — a broad sector index/ETF call would dilute the genuine alpha that sits in the hospital/diagnostics/CDMO cohort. Prefer selective names over the index.
🔬 Consumer Sector Analyst
neutral
Coverage 132 stocks · 60 recommending · Sector ARM 47.7 equal-wt / 52.8 float-wt
Consumer is two-speed: discretionary/retail (Titan, Trent, VBL) has analysts AND funds aligned with real growth, while classic staples (ITC, Asian Paints, Nestle) are seeing funds distribute even where ARM is high — net-neutral sector, lean discretionary-quality, avoid the slow-grower trap.
Working on
  • Reconciling the EW vs FF ARM gap (47.7 vs 52.8) — large-caps are carrying the tape while the median name is soft; checking if this is breadth-narrowing or a genuine megacap-quality tilt.
  • Vetting the Q3 bucket (44 names, the largest quadrant) where funds are buying AHEAD of analyst upgrades — Britannia (ARM 14, flow +2421), United Spirits (ARM 26, flow +1949), Naukri (ARM 42, flow +1413): is fund conviction early, or a value-trap to fade?
  • Stress-testing the QSR/quick-commerce flow surge (Eternal flow +7397, Lenskart +3500) against weak/zero earnings (Eternal PAT -31%, Lenskart PAT 0) — flow-driven, not earnings-driven; sizing accordingly.
  • Hotels micro-cycle: Leela PAT +760%, Chalet +351%, Samhi +558% — confirming this is a margin-recovery base effect vs a durable up-leg before routing the small-caps.
Pitches
accumulate TITAN ●●●3-6M→ Large Cap, Flexi Cap, Consumption
Cleanest large-cap discretionary compounder in the sector: analyst revisions, fund flow, and earnings all point the same way — a Quadrant-1 name with no signal conflict.
ARM 63 (above sector FF-ARM 52.8), 3M net fund flow +Rs221cr, PAT YoY +52.1%, FII change +15.65%, Quadrant 1. Mcap Rs3.24L cr.
add TRENT ●●●3-6M→ Large & Mid Cap, Consumption, Flexi Cap
Strongest flow-confirmed retail growth name in coverage — funds are accumulating hard and analysts are upgrading together, a rare both-positive setup at scale.
ARM 64, 3M net fund flow +Rs1,674cr (5th-largest accumulation in the desk), PAT YoY +12.1%, FII +15.59%, Quadrant 1. Mcap Rs1.73L cr.
accumulate VBL ●●○3-6M→ Large & Mid Cap, Consumption, Multi Cap

ARM 75 (top of the megacaps), 3M net fund flow +Rs1,062cr, PAT YoY +13.9%, FII +19.51%, Quadrant 1. Mcap Rs1.61L cr.
add TATACONSUM ●●○3-6M→ Large Cap, Flexi Cap, Consumption, Dividend Yield
Quadrant-1 staples turnaround — best earnings growth among the FMCG large-caps with analysts and funds both onside; a defensive way to own the sector without the distribution overhang on Nestle/Asian Paints.
ARM 62, 3M net fund flow +Rs439cr, PAT YoY +20.3% (vs Nestle +9.1, Godrej +0.5), FII +20.79%, Quadrant 1. Mcap Rs1.11L cr.
watch HONASA ●○○1-3M→ Small Cap, Mid Cap, Quant / Systematic
Small-cap with the strongest revision momentum in the desk plus explosive earnings recovery — high upside but micro-cap and a speculative ARM print; size only on a momentum sleeve.
ARM 100 (highest in coverage), PAT YoY +176.4%, 3M net fund flow +Rs108cr, FII +13.74%, Quadrant 1. Mcap only Rs9,245cr — small-cap.
Risks
  • ITC is the sector's red flag: ARM 52 but 3M flow -Rs2,217cr (largest distribution in the desk) and PAT -40.0% — large-cap funds holding it face an earnings + outflow squeeze; avoid adding.
  • ARM is a small tilt (IC ~0.03-0.045, 1-6M clock), not a per-name guarantee — high-ARM/funds-distributing names like Asian Paints (ARM 98 but flow -Rs1,150cr, Q2) and Nestle (ARM 89, flow -Rs135cr, Q2) show analysts and funds disagreeing; do not chase ARM alone.
  • Quick-commerce/QSR flow (Eternal +Rs7,397cr, Lenskart +Rs3,500cr) is running far ahead of earnings (Eternal PAT -30.6%, Lenskart PAT 0) — momentum/liquidity-driven, vulnerable to a sentiment reversal.
  • Hotel growth leaders (Leela +760%, Samhi +558%, Chalet +351% PAT) are low-base recovery prints, several in Q3 with weak ARM (Samhi 16) — base effects, not durable run-rate; treat as tactical not core.
🔬 Auto & Mobility Sector Analyst
neutral
Coverage 44 stocks · 17 recommending · Sector ARM 46.1 equal-wt / 47.6 float-wt
Auto is a split tape: analysts favour the component/ancillary complex (Bharat Forge ARM 84, Bosch 76, Eicher 71) where funds are quietly distributing, while mutual funds are aggressively buying the large OEMs (Maruti +Rs2,559cr, Hyundai +Rs1,811cr) that carry weak ARM — TVS Motor is the only name where analyst revisions and fund flow agree.
Working on
  • Resolving the OEM disagreement: funds put Rs4.4k cr into Maruti+Hyundai over 3m yet ARM sits at 40/43 and PAT is flat-to-down (Maruti +1.2%, Hyundai -3.7%) — is fund flow front-running a festive/volume turn the analysts haven't caught, or is it index-driven buying I should fade?
  • Stress-testing the ancillary 'analyst-ahead' cluster (Q2): Bharat Forge, Eicher, Bosch, Schaeffler, Motherson all show strong ARM but NET fund distribution (BharatForg -Rs368cr, Eicher -Rs339cr) — confirming whether the ARM upgrades are durable or already priced before I route them.
  • Screening the growth-vs-ARM mismatch: MRF (PAT +29.8% but ARM 5), CEAT (PAT +48% but ARM 3), UNO Minda (PAT +25.9% but ARM 16) — strong earnings yet bottom-decile revisions; checking if this is a margin/raw-material (tyre) de-rate the analysts see coming.
  • Tata Motors PV: PAT +193.6% YoY headline vs ARM 29 and the largest distribution in the book (-Rs869.9cr) — verifying the base-effect distortion before treating the growth number as real.
Pitches
accumulate TVSMOTOR ●●●3-6M→ Large Cap, Large & Mid Cap, Flexi Cap, Multi Cap, Consumption
The cleanest signal in my book: the only Quadrant-1 name where analyst revision-momentum and fund flow both point up. Premiumising 2W mix and EV traction are showing in the earnings line, and unlike the rest of Auto the funds are adding, not trimming.
ARM 66 (well above sector EW 46.1); net fund flow +Rs877.5cr (3m, accumulating); PAT +33.9% YoY; FII chg +22.56; Quadrant 1 (both analysts and funds positive); mcap Rs158,857cr.
watch BHARATFORG ●●○1-3M→ Quant / Systematic, Mid Cap, Infrastructure
Highest ARM in the entire desk — analysts are upgrading hard on the defence/forging and export recovery story. But funds are net sellers, so this is an analyst-led idea not yet confirmed by money. A momentum/quant desk can act on the revision tilt; discretionary desks should wait for flow to turn.
ARM 84 (top of sector, vs EW 46.1); PAT +19.1% YoY; FII chg +14.15; BUT net fund flow -Rs367.8cr (3m, distributing) — Quadrant 2 (analysts ahead of funds); mcap Rs61,070cr.
add EICHERMOT ●●○6-12M→ Large Cap, Flexi Cap, Focused, Consumption
Royal Enfield franchise with strong revision momentum and durable margins. A quality mid/large compounder where analysts are ahead — the -Rs339cr fund outflow looks like profit-taking against a high-ARM, high-FII name rather than a fundamental crack. Suitable for quality-tilted long-term desks.
ARM 71 (vs EW 46.1); PAT +16.5% YoY; FII chg +26.77 (one of the highest in the book); net fund flow -Rs338.5cr (3m); Quadrant 2; mcap Rs179,585cr.
hold MARUTI ●○○3-6M→ Large Cap, CIO
Mutual funds are buying the largest OEM hand over fist, but I will not lead with it: ARM is sub-50 and earnings are flat. This is fund conviction running ahead of the analyst signal — I flag it for the CIO and large-cap desks as a flow-driven call to size carefully, not an ARM-backed recommendation.
Net fund flow +Rs2,559.3cr (3m, the largest accumulation in the desk); FII chg +14.12; BUT ARM only 40 (below EW 46.1) and PAT just +1.2% YoY; Quadrant 3 (funds ahead of analysts); mcap Rs470,458cr.
watch SANSERA ●○○6-12M→ Small Cap, Mid Cap
Best earnings-plus-revision combination among smaller ancillaries — high ARM with 50%+ profit growth. The catch is a heavy fund outflow at a small mcap, so liquidity/flow risk is real; a small/mid-cap desk willing to look through the distribution gets a quality precision-forging grower.
ARM 75 (vs EW 46.1); PAT +50.2% YoY; FII chg +18.79; BUT net fund flow -Rs399.1cr (3m, distributing, large vs Rs9,107cr mcap); Quadrant 2.
Risks
  • Flow-vs-ARM divergence cuts both ways: the OEMs funds are buying (Maruti, Hyundai) have weak ARM (40/43) and near-flat PAT, while the ancillaries analysts like (Bharat Forge 84, Eicher 71, Bosch 76) are seeing net fund distribution — neither cohort has both signals aligned except TVS, so most pitches carry single-signal risk.
  • Tyre sub-sector is a red flag: MRF (ARM 5), CEAT (ARM 3) and JK Tyre (ARM 42, Q4) show bottom-decile revisions despite strong reported PAT (+29.8%/+48%/+52.2%) — likely a raw-material/margin de-rate the analysts see coming; avoid chasing the earnings prints.
  • ARM is a small ~1-6 month tilt (IC ~0.03-0.045), not a per-name guarantee — the high-ARM ancillary cluster is an analyst-revision bet that funds have NOT yet confirmed, so conviction must stay modest until flow turns.
  • Base-effect distortion: Tata Motors PV's +193.6% PAT YoY is flattered by a weak prior year; paired with ARM 29 and the book's largest outflow (-Rs869.9cr), the headline growth number is not investable on its own.
🔬 Industrials & Capital Goods Sector Analyst
neutral
Coverage 143 stocks · 61 recommending · Sector ARM 44.5 equal-wt / 39.2 float-wt
Capital-goods breadth is soft, not broken — float-weighted ARM just 39.2 (megacaps LT/ABB/Siemens all bottom-quartile drag it down), so I am NOT bullish the sector beta; the edge is in a thin band of quadrant-1 names where analyst revisions, fund flow AND earnings line up — solar/renewables and defence-adjacent — while I'd fade the crowded, no-revision megacaps.
Working on
  • Reconciling the quadrant-3 crowd (42 names funds are buying with no analyst revision behind them) — chiefly INDIGO (flow +3940 cr, but PAT -133% YoY) and ADANIPORTS — to separate genuine forward-earnings inflection from momentum chasing
  • Stress-testing the solar/renewables cluster (PREMIERENE, SUZLON, WAAREE, VIKRAMSOLR) for whether the ARM+flow+PAT alignment is durable into the order-book cycle or a crowded late-stage trade given FII positioning
  • Building the defence sub-desk view: BEL (ARM 67) and HAL (ARM 53, flow +2727) vs the ARM-1 collapse in BDL and MAZDOCK softness — is the revision cycle broadening or narrowing within defence
  • Watching the megacap divergence: LT/ABB/SIEMENS carry the sector index but show ARM 6/4/2 and net distribution — flagging whether this is a forward-numbers warning or just mean-reversion noise
Pitches
accumulate PREMIERENE (Premier Energies) ●●●3-6M→ Mid Cap, Infrastructure, Small Cap
Cleanest quadrant-1 setup in my coverage — analyst revisions, mutual-fund buying, and earnings all firing together in the solar manufacturing up-cycle. This is the highest-conviction triple-confirm name I have.
ARM 63 (upper tier), net fund flow +1994 cr over 3M (3rd-largest accumulation in the sector), PAT +61.2% YoY, quadrant 1. Mid-cap at 45,678 cr mcap.
add WAAREEENER (Waaree Energies) ●●●3-6M→ Mid Cap, Large & Mid Cap, Infrastructure
Best growth+revision combination in the renewables cluster — strongest earnings inflection of any quadrant-1 name, with analysts upgrading and funds still net buyers. The solar-manufacturing leader on fundamentals.
ARM 81 (top decile of coverage), PAT +101.5% YoY (the highest growth among quadrant-1 names), net fund flow +751 cr over 3M, quadrant 1. Large-mid at 92,778 cr.
add HAL (Hindustan Aeronautics) ●●○3-6M→ Large Cap, Large & Mid Cap, Infrastructure, Flexi Cap
The defence megacap where flow and revisions agree — large enough to size in core books, with funds aggressively accumulating ahead of the order cycle. Prefer it to BEL here because the money is moving, not just the estimates.
ARM 53, net fund flow +2727 cr over 3M (largest accumulation among large-caps in coverage), PAT +9.0% YoY, quadrant 1, FII +10.2%. Mcap 309,449 cr.
accumulate ENGINERSIN (Engineers India) ●●○1-3M→ Small Cap, Mid Cap, Quant / Systematic
Highest ARM in my entire 143-name coverage with funds also net buyers and double-digit earnings growth — a clean small-cap quadrant-1 revision story on the hydrocarbon/infra engineering order book. A short-clock ARM tilt, sized small.
ARM 97 (#1 in coverage), net fund flow +213 cr over 3M, PAT +18.9% YoY, quadrant 1. Small-cap at 11,603 cr.
reduce LT (Larsen & Toubro) ●●○1-3M→ CIO, Large Cap, Infrastructure, Flexi Cap
The sector bellwether is a quadrant-4 warning: analysts are NOT revising up and funds are net sellers, despite it anchoring every infra/large-cap index. Don't add on index weight alone — the forward numbers and the smart money both say wait. Flagging to CIO given its benchmark heft.
ARM 6 (bottom quartile, lowest among megacaps), net fund flow -1478 cr over 3M (2nd-largest distribution in sector), PAT only +7.2% YoY, quadrant 4. Largest mcap in coverage at 518,763 cr.
Risks
  • ARM is a small, short-clock tilt (IC ~0.03-0.045, 1-6M) — none of these are per-name guarantees; size accordingly and re-check at the next revision cycle
  • Crowding/late-cycle risk in solar/renewables: PREMIERENE, SUZLON, WAAREE are all quadrant-1 simultaneously — if the order-book narrative cracks, the same funds that accumulated can unwind together (correlated bet, limited true breadth)
  • Quadrant-3 trap: 42 names (the largest bucket) have funds buying with NO analyst revision support — INDIGO especially (flow +3940 cr but PAT -133% YoY) — treat fund flow alone as momentum, not validated alpha
  • Megacap mean-reversion risk on the LT reduce call: ARM 6 could simply be revision noise on a quality compounder, so this is a fade-the-add signal, not a sell-quality thesis
🔬 Energy & Power Sector Analyst
neutral
Coverage 37 stocks · 14 recommending · Sector ARM 46.1 equal-wt / 48.0 float-wt
Energy is net-soft at the index level (EW ARM 46.1 / FF ARM 48.0, only 14 of 37 names recommending), but it is a stock-picker's sector right now: a clean upstream/refining earnings-recovery cluster (ONGC ARM 98, MRPL, Chennai Petro) sits in Quadrant 1 with both analysts and funds aligned, while the biggest quadrant is 3 (15 names) where funds are accumulating ahead of any analyst upgrade.
Working on
  • Resolving the Quadrant-3 tension in OMCs: HPCL and IOC show explosive PAT YoY (+167.9% / +216.8%) and HPCL drew +Rs1,120cr of fund flow, yet both sit at rock-bottom ARM (2 and 5) - deciding whether this is a marketing-margin earnings spike analysts are right to fade, or an upgrade cycle about to turn.
  • Stress-testing the ONGC / MRPL / Chennai Petro upstream-refining call - ARM 98/79/94 plus PAT YoY of +29.9% / +3,336% / +1,350% all agree, but the small-cap refiners' growth is off a tiny crude-cracking base; quantifying how much is durable vs one-off inventory gains.
  • Reconciling the NTPC signal split (ARM 65, Quadrant 2) against the largest fund outflow on my desk (-Rs2,696cr) - testing whether funds are simply rotating out of a fairly-valued utility while the upgrade momentum is real.
  • Watching the renewables cohort divergence: Adani Green (ARM 75, +Rs885cr flow, Q1) is bid while NTPC Green (ARM 3) and ACME Solar are not - sizing whether this is a quality/execution gap or sentiment.
Pitches
accumulate ONGC ●●●1-3M→ Large Cap, Value & Contra, Multi Cap, Quant / Systematic
Best risk-reward on the desk: a large-cap upstream name where analyst revision-momentum is maxed out and earnings are accelerating, with funds already net buyers - the rare large-cap Quadrant 1 setup, so the small ARM tilt is reinforced rather than fighting the tape.
ARM 98 (highest on a Rs3.0L cr-mcap name; sector EW ARM only 46.1), PAT YoY +29.9%, net fund flow +Rs260cr (Quadrant 1, both signals positive), FII change +7.97%.
add MRPL ●●○1-3M→ Small Cap, Mid Cap, Quant / Systematic
Small-cap refining earnings-inflection with analysts upgrading hard and a Quadrant 1 confirmation - a high-torque expression of the same upstream/refining recovery as ONGC, sized for a higher-beta sleeve.
ARM 79, PAT YoY +3,335.7% (off a small base - mostly cracking-margin/inventory recovery, treat as torque not trend), net flow +Rs17cr (Quadrant 1), mcap Rs25,243cr, FII change +3.41%.
hold RELIANCE ●●○3-6M→ Large Cap, Flexi Cap, Multi Cap, Focused
The desk's anchor: funds are the clear leaders here (largest accumulation on my desk) with solid earnings, but analyst revisions are only neutral - own it for the flow and franchise, do not lean on it for the ARM tilt.
Largest fund inflow on the desk +Rs3,800.6cr and strongest FII change +18.67%, PAT YoY +17.8%, but ARM only 49 (Quadrant 3 - funds ahead of analysts); mcap Rs19.7L cr.
watch COALINDIA ●○○1-3M→ Dividend Yield, Value & Contra, Large Cap
Strong analyst upgrade momentum and a Quadrant 1 tag, but earnings are actually contracting - a case where the ARM tilt and the fundamentals disagree, so I want fund desks to size small until the PAT trend confirms the revisions.
ARM 85 with net flow +Rs138cr (Quadrant 1), but PAT YoY -12.0%; mcap Rs2.37L cr, FII change +8.38%.
avoid HINDPETRO ●●○1-3M→ CIO, Value & Contra
Classic Quadrant 3 trap to flag for CIO: huge reported earnings and heavy fund buying, but analyst revision-momentum is at the floor (ARM 2) - the Street is fading this OMC profit spike as a marketing-margin windfall, and I side with the revisions over the chase.
PAT YoY +167.9% and net flow +Rs1,120.2cr (funds accumulating), yet ARM 2 (Quadrant 3, near the bottom of the desk); same pattern in IOC (PAT YoY +216.8%, ARM 5, flow -Rs876cr). FII change +17.26%.
Risks
  • ARM is a small, short-horizon tilt (IC ~0.03-0.045, 1-6M clock) - the ONGC/MRPL/Coal India ARM leadership is a probabilistic edge, not a per-name guarantee, and is sensitive to a crude-price reversal that would re-rate every upstream/refining call at once.
  • The OMC Quadrant-3 cluster (HPCL, IOC, BPCL) is a genuine signal conflict: blow-out PAT YoY (+168% to +217%) and fund buying versus bottom-decile ARM (2-5). If analysts are wrong to fade it, avoiding these forfeits real momentum; if funds are wrong, the flow reverses sharply.
  • Earnings-growth headline numbers for the small refiners (MRPL +3,336%, Chennai Petro +1,350%) are off tiny/negative prior-year bases - genuine cross-stock comparability is poor; do not size on the growth rank alone.
  • Concentration/policy risk: the desk is dominated by PSUs and the Adani complex (Adani Green/Power/Energy Solutions) - regulatory, fuel-cost pass-through, and single-group governance shocks can hit multiple holdings together, so de-correlation across these names is lower than the names count suggests.

neutral
Coverage 78 stocks · 30 recommending · Sector ARM 47.2 equal-wt / 53.4 float-wt
Materials is a two-speed tape: only 30/78 names recommending, sector ARM middling (EW 47.2 / float-wt 53.4), and the biggest single bucket is Q3 (funds buying ahead of analysts, 25 names) plus Q4 dead-weight (23). Working on
  • Reconciling the Q3 cluster (25 names) where MFs are accumulating but ARM has NOT confirmed (UltraTech ARM 29, JSW ARM 40, Ambuja ARM 2, SRF ARM 11) — is the money early or is the Street right to hold back?

  • Cement sub-sector dispersion: UltraTech/Shree/Ramco PAT all up 35-158% YoY but ARM scattered 22-50, while Ambuja (ARM 2) and ACC (ARM 4) are the sector's worst-revised — sorting cyclical-recovery winners from laggards.
  • Validating the metals PAT spike (JSW +630%, Tata +243%) as a low-base cyclical artifact vs a durable upgrade — ARM is NOT confirming either (40 and 49), so I treat the earnings number as backward-looking.

ARM 98 (joint sector-high), Q1, net flow +97 cr, PAT +14.1% YoY, FII +13.6%, mcap 65,186 cr.
avoid VEDL ●●●immediate→ CIO, Value & Contra
Loudest divergence on my desk and the one to escalate: ARM is healthy (70) but mutual funds dumped -13,051 cr — by an order of magnitude the sector's largest distribution. Flow this large against a decent ARM is a red flag (de-merger/overhang risk the Street has not yet marked down); do NOT add until the flow reverses.
Net flow -13,051 cr (largest in coverage by 5x), ARM 70, Q2, PAT +22.2% YoY, FII +13.9%, mcap 188,352 cr.
Risks
  • Commodity beta dominates this desk — ARM (IC ~0.03-0.045, 1-6M clock) is a small tilt and can be swamped by a global metals/steel-price or China-demand swing; treat every metals pitch as cyclical, size accordingly.
  • The headline PAT spikes in steel (JSW +630%, Tata +243%) are off a depressed base and NOT confirmed by ARM (40, 49) — do not read them as forward upgrades.
  • Q3 is the biggest bucket (25 names): funds are buying ahead of analysts in large positions (UltraTech, JSW, SRF). If ARM never confirms, that flow can reverse fast — confirmation risk on any Q3 name.
  • Vedanta's -13,051 cr outflow signals a possible event/overhang the ARM has not caught yet; the analyst-revision signal can lag a corporate-action shock, so the flow read should override ARM here.
🔬 Realty & Infrastructure Sector Analyst
constructive
Coverage 15 stocks · 9 recommending · Sector ARM 46.9 equal-wt / 50.9 float-wt
Realty is a stock-picker's tape, not a sector bet — sector ARM is only middling (EW 46.9 / float-weighted 50.9, recommending 9 of 15) but a tight cluster of large-caps has analysts, mutual-fund flow and earnings all pulling the same way; lean into the Q1 names, fade the Q4 laggards.
Working on
  • Resolving the DLF divergence: funds put in +2,894.6 cr of net flow (by far the sector's biggest) yet ARM sits at only 51 and PAT is flat (+1.1% YoY) — deciding whether flow is leading an analyst upgrade or chasing index weight.
  • Watching the ANANTRAJ contradiction: ARM 82 (sector's highest) but funds distributed -52.9 cr — testing whether MFs are fading a stretched upgrade or just booking profits on a small-cap.
  • Stress-testing the SIGNATURE +983.2% PAT print — confirming it's a low-base optical vs durable pre-sales momentum before sizing it as anything more than context (ARM only 59, flow +5.8 cr is negligible).
  • Building a commercial/annuity vs residential split — Phoenix (malls) and DLF (annuity + dev) screen differently from pure residential developers and may deserve separate flow benchmarks.

Cleanest Q1 setup in the sector: analyst revision-momentum, mutual-fund accumulation and earnings are all aligned, making it the highest-quality way to own the residential-cycle upturn at scale.

accumulate PHOENIXLTD ●●●3-6M→ Large & Mid Cap, Consumption, Infrastructure, Flexi Cap
Best annuity/mall play on the desk — recurring rental cash flow with the largest FII step-up in coverage and clean Q1 alignment; a lower-beta way to own the real-estate theme than pure residential developers.
ARM 53, net fund flow +606.8 cr (2nd-largest in sector), PAT +19.0% YoY, FII +32.97 (highest in coverage), mcap Rs 57,973 cr, Quadrant 1.
add LODHA ●●○1-3M→ Large Cap, Large & Mid Cap, CIO
Q2 analyst-ahead large-cap: strong revision-momentum and heavy FII buying that mutual funds have not yet chased — an early-mover entry before MF flow confirms.
ARM 68 (3rd-highest), FII +21.24 (2nd-highest), PAT +24.0% YoY, but net MF flow only -5.6 cr (flat, funds not yet in), mcap Rs 120,401 cr, Quadrant 2.
hold DLF ●●○1-3M→ Large Cap, Infrastructure, CIO
Funds are accumulating aggressively but the analyst signal and earnings have not confirmed — own a benchmark weight, but wait for ARM to turn up before adding on the flow alone; flow into a flat-earnings, mid-ARM mega-cap can be index-driven rather than conviction.
Net fund flow +2,894.6 cr (largest in sector by far) but ARM only 51 and PAT flat at +1.1% YoY; FII +13.54, mcap Rs 187,715 cr (sector's largest), Quadrant 1.
avoid GODREJPROP ●●○1-3M→ Large & Mid Cap, Mid Cap
Clear Q4 laggard — analysts are cutting and funds are selling despite decent reported growth; the largest FII position here looks like a crowded long unwinding, not a setup to add into.
ARM 16 (3rd-lowest in coverage), net fund flow -21.8 cr, PAT +32.5% YoY (not enough to stem the de-rating), FII +26.17, mcap Rs 63,669 cr, Quadrant 4.
Risks
  • Realty is high-beta and rate-sensitive — the whole desk's ARM tilt unwinds fast if rate-cut expectations reverse; ARM is a small 1-6M edge (IC ~0.03-0.045), not a per-name guarantee.
  • Flow-vs-ARM divergences are unresolved: DLF (+2,894.6 cr flow, ARM 51) and ANANTRAJ (ARM 82, flow -52.9 cr) point in opposite directions — at least one signal is wrong and could whipsaw a position.
  • Headline growth is distorted by low bases / lumpy revenue recognition (SIGNATURE +983.2%, SOBHA +104.2%, EMBDL -578.1%) — PAT YoY is unreliable for sizing in this sector; lean on ARM and flow, not the earnings print.
  • Sector breadth is thin: 4 of 15 names sit in Q4 (both weak) and EW ARM is sub-50, so this is a narrow large-cap call — small-cap longs here carry liquidity and single-project execution risk.
🔬 Telecom & Media Sector Analyst
constructive
Coverage 19 stocks · 10 recommending · Sector ARM 59.4 equal-wt / 55.9 float-wt
Telecom & Media is two sectors in one tape: a flow-and-revision-led media/content + digital sleeve (NAZARA ARM 98, SAREGAMA 72, TIPSMUSIC 73, SUNTV ARM 64) where analysts and funds agree, sitting on top of a heavy-telecom block (Indus ARM 35, Idea 30, Tata Comm 21) that funds are buying but analysts are NOT upgrading — sector ARM is 59.4 equal-weight but only 55.9 float-weight, so my conviction is in the smaller Quadrant-1 names, not the index-cap incumbents.
Working on
  • Decomposing the 6 Quadrant-3 names (funds buying, ARM lagging): Indus +Rs938cr/PAT -28%, Idea +Rs56cr, Tata Comm +Rs286cr/PAT -46%, Zee +Rs7cr/PAT -60%, HFCL +Rs121cr/PAT +89% — separating real turnaround (HFCL) from passive/index flow into deteriorating incumbents (Indus/Tata Comm/Zee).
  • Stress-testing Bharti Airtel: dominant flow (+Rs8,521cr/3m) and FII +27.8 with ARM only 60 and PAT YoY -9.8 — is the soft PAT optical (one-offs/Africa FX) or a revision risk that the +Rs8.5k cr inflow is front-running?
  • Validating the content/music compounder cluster (SAREGAMA, TIPSMUSIC, NAZARA) as a coherent Quadrant-1 theme with durable ARM, vs. a small-cap momentum crowd that the 1-6M ARM clock will mean-revert.
  • Flagging the deep-laggard tail (ZEEL ARM 2 / PAT -60.1; Tata Comm ARM 21 / PAT -46; Tejas Net PAT -303.8) as avoid/short-watch despite some opportunistic FII additions (Zee FII +25.3).
Pitches
accumulate NAZARA ●●○1-3M→ Small Cap, Mid Cap, Technology & Digital, Quant / Systematic
Cleanest Quadrant-1 in my book: analysts and funds both moving the same way on a profitable gaming/digital-media platform — the highest-conviction ARM signal in the sector that is also backed by real earnings, not just momentum.
ARM 98 (sector ARM-leader #2), net fund flow +Rs72.5cr/3m (Quadrant 1), PAT YoY +41.4%, FII +13.3; mcap Rs10,381cr (small/mid).
add BHARTIARTL ●●○3-6M→ Large Cap, Flexi Cap, Multi Cap, CIO
Sector anchor with overwhelming smart-money flow and rising analyst revisions; the soft headline PAT looks optical against a +Rs8.5k cr/3m active-fund bid and the strongest FII build in coverage — a core large-cap hold, but I want the PAT delta checked before sizing up.
Net fund flow +Rs8,520.6cr/3m (largest in sector by ~9x), ARM 60 (Quadrant 1), FII +27.8 (highest in coverage), PAT YoY -9.8 (the one caveat); mcap Rs11.96 lakh cr.
accumulate SAREGAMA ●●○3-6M→ Mid Cap, Small Cap, Flexi Cap, Consumption
Music-IP annuity compounder in Quadrant 1 — strong analyst revisions with positive (if modest) fund flow and steady earnings; a defensive content-royalty model rather than a telecom-capex bet.

watch HFCL ●○○1-3M→ Small Cap, Mid Cap, Infrastructure
Best earnings momentum in the sector with rising ARM and active fund buying, but it sits in Quadrant 3 (funds ahead of analysts) — I want the analyst revisions to confirm before upgrading; the +89% PAT and +Rs121cr inflow make it the most interesting telecom-equipment turnaround to track.
PAT YoY +89.1% (sector growth-leader #1), ARM 64, net fund flow +Rs120.6cr/3m (Quadrant 3 — flow ahead of revisions), FII +7.1; mcap Rs10,680cr.
avoid ZEEL ●●○1-3M→ Value & Contra, CIO
Deepest revision laggard in coverage with collapsing earnings; opportunistic FII additions and a token fund flow do not offset ARM near the floor — avoid despite the optically low price.

Risks
  • ARM is a small tilt (IC ~0.03-0.045) on a 1-6M clock, not a per-name guarantee — the small-cap content cluster (NAZARA ARM 98, PVRINOX 80, STLTECH 100) is exactly where high ARM can mean-revert fast; size for that.
  • Float-weighted sector ARM (55.9) trails equal-weight (59.4) because the largest telecom incumbents (Indus 35, Idea 30, Tata Comm 21) are the weakest names — any index/passive-driven view of the sector is structurally heavier in the laggards I'd avoid.
  • Quadrant-3 flow into deteriorating incumbents (Indus +Rs938cr with PAT -28%; Tata Comm +Rs286cr with PAT -46%) may be index/passive or scheme-inflow deployment, NOT active conviction — do not read it as a buy signal without ARM confirmation.
  • Bharti Airtel's PAT YoY -9.8 against a record +Rs8.5k cr inflow is the key tension: if the soft PAT is structural rather than one-off, the flow is front-running a revision that hasn't fully landed (ARM only 60, not extreme).

neutral
Coverage 288 stocks · 104 recommending · Sector ARM 52.8 equal-wt / 52.7 float-wt
Diversified sits dead on the midline (sector ARM 52.8 EW / 52.7 FF) with 43% of names (125/288) in the both-weak Quadrant 4 and only 13% (37) in the both-positive Quadrant 1 — there is no sector tailwind to ride, so the entire edge is single-name selection where analyst revisions, fund flow and earnings actually line up.
Working on
  • Mining the 37 Quadrant-1 names (analysts AND funds both positive) for the handful where ARM, 3M flow and PAT growth all confirm — that overlap is where the IC lives in a flat sector
  • Separating real earnings INFLECTIONS from optical base-effect spikes in growth_leaders (DCAL +3133%, SIS +1050%, TATVA +740%, ASHIANA +561%) — only MAHLIFE (PAT +380.6%, ARM 93) and SIMPLEXINF (PAT +272.7%, ARM 98) pair high growth with a high analyst revision
  • Watching heavy fund distribution in otherwise-decent names — SUNDRMFAST flow -291.7cr, SBCL -204cr (ARM 75), GREENLAM -183.7cr, SANOFI -111.3cr (ARM 68) — to flag de-rating risk before it shows in price
  • Pruning the long tail of Quadrant-4 ARM laggards (KOLTEPATIL PAT -136%, EPACK -94.1%, GREENPANEL -44.4%) off any buy list — both analysts and funds are exiting and earnings confirm the weakness

Contentious because trailing PAT is still negative, so this is a forward bet, not a trailing-quality bet — routing to CIO.
ARM 100 (sector max vs sector 52.8), flow_3m +64.8 cr, FII +10.57 (highest among large-caps in coverage), Quadrant 1, mcap 29,365 cr; OFFSET: PAT YoY -10.6% (the reason it is a turn call, not a compounder).

Earnings-inflection special situation in real estate where analysts are clearly leading (Quadrant 2) — revisions are near-maxed on a large PAT jump, but funds have NOT yet stepped in, so this is the analysts-ahead setup to size into on the first sign of flow confirmation.

ARM 77 (well above sector 52.8), PAT YoY +23.2%, FII +3.52, Quadrant 2; OFFSET: flow_3m -73.4 cr (funds distributing), mcap 35,782 cr.
Risks
  • ARM is a SMALL tilt (IC ~0.03-0.045) on a 1-6 month clock, not a per-name guarantee — in a sector this flat (43% of names in Quadrant 4) the hit rate on any single ARM-100 name is modest, so size as a basket, not a conviction single.
  • Turn calls carry trailing-earnings risk: GUJGASLTD (PAT -10.6%) is a forward recovery bet — if the earnings inflection slips, ARM-100 can reverse quickly with no fundamental floor underneath.
  • growth_leaders are dominated by base-effect optics (DCAL +3133%, SIS +1050%, TATVA +740%) where the headline PAT YoY overstates the real run-rate — I am deliberately NOT pitching those despite the eye-catching numbers.
  • Heavy fund DISTRIBUTION in names that still screen well on ARM (SBCL ARM 75 flow -204cr, GANECOS ARM 77 flow -145.6cr & PAT -62.5%, SANOFI ARM 68 flow -111.3cr) signals smart money may be reading a de-rating analysts have not caught — a counter-signal to the ARM tilt that warrants caution on the analyst-led Quadrant-2 cohort.
📈 Large Cap Core Equity · Fund Manager
balanced
33 peer funds · category median IR 0.49 · bench NIFTY 100
exemplar WhiteOak Capital Large Cap Fund - Regular (G): IR 1.31, IC -0.024 (t -1.1), TE 2.5% — skilled
Benchmark NIFTY 100; category median IR 0.49; exemplar tracking error 2.5-3.1% and the honest lesson from my own desk stats is that exemplar revealed IC is ~0 to slightly negative (ICICI Pru ic_mean +0.009 t=0.7; Mirae +0.007 t=0.6; WhiteOak -0.024 t=-1.1) -- their +3.4-3.7%/yr excess comes from low-TE construction and breadth, NOT heroic stock-picking.
